g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Caches and Memory Management Units in modern computing systems.
The memory wall: processors have become much faster than memory, creating a critical bottleneck in system performance.
g.text: Processor speeds increased dramatically over decades
Memory access times did NOT keep pace
This speed gap creates a performance bottleneck!
Cache solves this by providing fast storage!

g.data-narration: Cache memory is high-speed SRAM between CPU and main memory that stores frequently accessed data to reduce latency.
Cache has three main components: the controller manages operations, SRAM stores data, and tag memory tracks addresses.
There are three cache mapping techniques: direct, fully associative, and set-associative - each with trade-offs.
In direct mapping, each memory block maps to exactly one cache line. Simple but can cause conflict misses.
g.text: Each main memory block maps to ONE specific cache line

g.data-narration: Fully associative mapping allows any block in any cache line - most flexible but requires expensive content-addressable memory.
g.text: Any memory block can go in ANY cache line - maximum flexibility
✓ Best hit rate | ✗ Expensive CAM hardware needed

g.data-narration: Set-associative is a compromise: cache is divided into sets, each with multiple lines - like 4-way meaning 4 choices per set.
g.text: Cache divided into SETS, each set has multiple WAYS (lines)

g.data-narration: AMAT formula: Average Memory Access Time equals hit time plus miss rate times miss penalty. This measures cache efficiency.
g.text: AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)
Example: Ht=2, Miss=8%, Mp=20 → AMAT = 2 + (0.08×20) = 3.6 cycles

g.data-narration: Write-through updates both cache and memory simultaneously. Write-back updates only cache first, memory on eviction.
g.text: Write to BOTH cache and main memory
Write to cache first, memory on eviction

g.data-narration: A cache line contains valid bit, dirty bit, tag address, and the actual data storage - typically 32-64 bytes.
g.text: • Valid: Is data current? | Dirty: Modified? | Tag: Memory address | Data: Actual bytes

g.data-narration: The Memory Management Unit translates virtual addresses to physical addresses and provides memory protection.
g.text: Hardware that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses with protection
Found in Cortex-A (Linux-capable) | NOT in most Cortex-M microcontrollers

g.data-narration: The Translation Lookaside Buffer is a special cache storing recent virtual-to-physical address translations for fast lookup.
g.text: Avoids page table lookup on every memory access!

g.data-narration: Remember these key formulas: AMAT, cache lines calculation, offset bits, index bits, and tag bits for address mapping.
g.text: AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)
Cache Lines = Cache Size ÷ Line Size

g.data-narration: Key exam tips: memorize AMAT formula, understand address mapping, know when to use write policies, and remember MMU functions.
g.text: 1. Cache mapping trade-offs: Direct=simple, Associative=flexible, Set-Associative=balanced
2. AMAT formula is frequently asked - memorize: AMAT = Ht + (Mr × Mp)
3. For address mapping: divide address into Tag, Index, Offset bits
4. MMU: Address translation, Memory protection, Access control

g.data-narration: Thank you for learning about Caches and MMUs. These concepts are essential for advanced embedded systems development.
g.text: Thank you for studying this topic! Keep practicing cache problems.
